coaching.coaching: Pin down reviewer_id attribution verification contract with a concrete worked example.
coaching.subject: reviewer_id attribution verification contract

coaching.coaching: Plan how acceptance criteria coverage gaps and verifiability will be verified in-session — restate any deferred or unobservable success target as an observable proxy.
coaching.subject: acceptance criteria coverage gaps and verifiability

coaching.coaching: Pin down threshold callback signature and binding contract with a concrete worked example.
coaching.subject: threshold callback signature and binding contract

coaching.coaching: Record an ADR for ambiguous-gate error class and raise contract — it reads like a one-way door.
coaching.subject: ambiguous-gate error class and raise contract

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a check to AC2 (or a dedicated AC) asserting that each emitted project finding carries `reviewer_id` equal to the physical prompt id (e.g., extend the `-k fan_in or eval` pytest assertion to inspect `finding['reviewer_id']`).
evidence: 'On each emitted project finding, overwrite `criteria` with `[logical_id]` and set `reviewer_id` to the physical prompt id.'
AC2 verifies only: 'retains its logical id in every finding's `criteria`' — no mention of `reviewer_id` in any AC or verify command.
findings.finding: The Scope section specifies that on each emitted project finding the runner must 'set `reviewer_id` to the physical prompt id', but no AC verifies this attribution. The `reviewer_id` field is a named deliverable with observable test-time behavior, yet it appears in none of the pytest verify commands.
findings.location: Scope section (physical-to-logical map paragraph); Acceptance Criteria AC2
scenarios: An implementer ships the `criteria` overwrite but omits the `reviewer_id` field; all ACs pass, but the deliverable is incomplete and Pass 3 attribution silently breaks.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC2: '…and each project finding's `reviewer_id` equals the physical prompt id (e.g., `code-review-project-review-phase-boundaries`); verify with the same test module using an assertion on the finding dict.'

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that child AC1 test coverage includes at least one 'eval'-matching test for runner-side validator-safe repository prompt/eval id resolution (matching parent AC2's `-k 'fan_in or eval'`), or update parent AC2's verify filter to `-k fan_in` and move the id-validation assertion explicitly into child AC1's description.
evidence: Parent AC2 verify: `.venv/bin/pytest -q tests/unit/test_code_review_project_criteria.py -k 'fan_in or eval'`
Child AC1 verify: `.venv/bin/pytest -q tests/unit/test_code_review_project_criteria.py -k fan_in`
Child AC4 verify: `.venv/bin/pytest -q tests/unit/test_code_review_project_criteria.py -k 'eval or cli'` — targets CLI resolver, not runner-side prompt/eval id validation
Parent AC2 text: 'resolves validator-safe repository prompt/eval ids' — this is a runner-side assertion, not a CLI eval assertion
container pairing: parent + 8a4b-e8b6-1bf9-48d8
findings.finding: Child AC1's verify filter `-k fan_in` is narrower than parent AC2's `-k 'fan_in or eval'`: the 'eval' sub-filter in parent AC2 targets runner-side validator-safe repository prompt/eval id resolution, but child AC1 omits it. Child AC4's 'eval or cli' filter covers the CLI resolver, not runner-side prompt/eval id validation. No child AC explicitly verifies that the runner-side project prompt/eval asset resolution is tested under an 'eval'-keyed test case, leaving the 'eval' half of parent AC2's verify command unclaimed.
findings.location: child 8a4b-e8b6-1bf9-48d8 — Acceptance Criteria AC1 vs parent AC2
scenarios: A test named 'test_project_eval_id_validation' (tagged 'eval') that verifies runner-side prompt/eval asset resolution exists in the parent's mental model but is absorbed into child AC4's CLI-focused 'eval or cli' filter, creating ambiguity about what 'eval' means in each context.

If the runner-side prompt/eval id validation tests are not written (because no child AC explicitly claims them), parent AC2 will fail its completion verifier on the 'eval' sub-filter even if fan-in tests pass.
findings.suggested_fix: Expand child AC1 to read: '…every emitted finding carries the logical id in `criteria`, the physical prompt/eval ids pass validation, and runner-side asset resolution is covered under test names matching both `fan_in` and `eval`; verify with `-k 'fan_in or eval'`.' This aligns the child's verify command with the parent's and makes runner-side eval coverage explicit.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the contract for `criterion_prompt_id`/`calibrate_criterion` when `gate_key` is neither `"plan_review"` nor `"code_review"` (e.g., 'raise `ValueError` with a descriptive message') so executors don't need to guess.
evidence: 'Extend `criteria.ids.criterion_prompt_id(criterion_id, *, gate_key="plan_review")` with `CODE_REVIEW_PROMPT_PREFIX = "code-review-"`' — only two known gate values are illustrated, no else-branch contract stated
ABSENCE: no mention of ValueError, assertion, or fallback for unknown gate_key values
findings.finding: The behavior of `criterion_prompt_id` and `calibrate_criterion` when `gate_key` receives a value other than `"plan_review"` or `"code_review"` is unspecified; an executor must guess whether to raise, default to plan-review, or propagate the unknown key to the prompt prefix logic.
scenarios: A caller passes a misspelled gate key (e.g. `gate_key='codereview'`); the executor doesn't know whether to silently produce a plan-review id, raise immediately, or propagate a bad prefix.
Calibration integration tests that probe error paths will differ across implementations if the contract is unstated.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the scope paragraph: 'Any other `gate_key` value raises `ValueError(f"Unknown gate_key: {gate_key!r}")`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Broaden the rollback trigger to cover both overlay-absent and overlay-present regressions in `code_review_decide`, or add an explicit AC test that validates threshold/nit-suppression parity for overlay-present repos against the pre-change behavior.
evidence: Plan quote: 'Revert the fan-in, routing lookup, eval resolution, tests, and documentation together if overlay-absent compatibility regresses'
Plan quote (Scope): 'In `code_review_decide`, compute `routing = registry.effective_routing(ctx.repo_root)` once … no YAML input or LLM step is added'
The `code_review_decide` routing change applies to every repository regardless of overlay presence
findings.finding: The rollback trigger is scoped exclusively to 'overlay-absent compatibility regresses,' but the `code_review_decide` change—computing `effective_routing` once for both threshold and nit-suppression—affects all repositories running code review, not just overlay-absent ones. A regression in an overlay-present repo (e.g., incorrect threshold computed for a repo that has a `.rebar/` overlay) would not satisfy the stated rollback condition, leaving that class of breakage without an explicit back-out signal.
scenarios: A repo with a `.rebar/` overlay receives a wrong block_threshold because `effective_routing` returns different data than the previous per-call path; the rollback condition 'overlay-absent compatibility regresses' is false so no revert is triggered.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the rollback sentence to: 'Revert the fan-in, routing lookup, eval resolution, tests, and documentation together if overlay-absent *or* overlay-present compatibility regresses (verified by the four-pass regression suite).' Add a parity sub-case to `test_code_review_project_criteria.py` that asserts `code_review_decide` produces identical threshold and nit-suppression decisions for an overlay-present repo before and after the routing refactor.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `reviewer_id` (or equivalent phrasing describing the physical-id attribution on project findings) to the `docs/llm-framework.md` 'Code-review project criteria' section and include it in the AC verification assertion.
evidence: Plan quote: 'On each emitted project finding, overwrite `criteria` with `[logical_id]` and set `reviewer_id` to the physical prompt id.'
AC doc assertion checks: 'Code-review project criteria', 'round_a', 'criteria eval', 'configuration error', 'effective routing' — `reviewer_id` is absent
Pass 3 and downstream reporting tools that consume findings need to understand the logical/physical split in `reviewer_id` vs `criteria`
findings.finding: The finding-attribution contract—`reviewer_id` set to the physical prompt id on every emitted project criterion finding—is a new behavioral contract for downstream consumers (Pass 3, reporting, any tooling that reads `reviewer_id`) but is not listed among the strings the doc AC assertion verifies in `docs/llm-framework.md`.
findings.location: Scope section (finding attribution paragraph) and AC line 6 (docs verification)
scenarios: A criterion author or tool author reads the docs to understand finding output shape; they see logical ids in `criteria` but do not learn that `reviewer_id` carries the physical id, causing incorrect filtering or attribution logic.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the AC Python assertion to also check `'reviewer_id'` (or a prose phrase like `'physical prompt id'`) in the doc string, and add a sentence to the new doc section: 'Each project-criterion finding carries `reviewer_id` set to the physical prompt id (e.g., `code-review-project-review-phase-boundaries`) while `criteria` contains only the logical id (e.g., `project.review-phase-boundaries`), ensuring Pass 3 and reporting tools can reference either form.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the full switching rule for `criterion_prompt_id` under `gate_key="code_review"`: does it apply `CODE_REVIEW_PROMPT_PREFIX` to ALL ids (built-ins and project.*) or only to `project.*`-prefixed ids, and what happens for a non-project built-in id passed with `gate_key="code_review"`?
evidence: Plan: 'Extend `criteria.ids.criterion_prompt_id(criterion_id, *, gate_key="plan_review")` with `CODE_REVIEW_PROMPT_PREFIX = "code-review-"`; the default preserves plan-review callers, while `gate_key="code_review"` produces the physical project id.'
Current ids.py has no gate_key parameter and maps every id to `plan-review-<id>`.
The plan names only the `project.<name>` → `code-review-project-<name>` mapping for code_review gate; behavior for a bare built-in id like 'security' passed with gate_key='code_review' is not stated.

calibrate_criterion in eval.py calls criterion_prompt_id for the prompt_id used to load the eval spec — if the switching rule is wrong for non-project ids, the eval spec load silently targets the wrong path.
findings.finding: The plan introduces `criterion_prompt_id(criterion_id, *, gate_key="plan_review")` with a `code_review` branch but never specifies what the function returns for a non-`project.*` built-in id (e.g. `'security'`) when `gate_key="code_review"` — the switching rule is incomplete.
scenarios: A caller passes `criterion_prompt_id('security', gate_key='code_review')` — it is ambiguous whether this returns `code-review-security` or `plan-review-security` or raises.
calibrate_criterion with gate_key='code_review' and a built-in overlay id would load the wrong .eval.yaml file if the prefix switching is not fully specified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the `repo_root=None` fallback for the new `_code_review_prompt_id(prompt_id, repo_root)` — e.g. 'when repo_root is None, fall back to the existing closed-set validation (base/verify/OVERLAY_IDS), preserving pre-change behavior for built-in overlays.'
evidence: Plan: 'Extend `_code_review_prompt_id(prompt_id, repo_root)` to recognize the sanitized physical id only when its logical inverse is active, then reuse the existing `_run_code_review_case` arm.'

Plan: '`_code_review_prompt_id` therefore receives `repo_root`, builds an active logical-to-physical map with `criterion_prompt_id(..., gate_key="code_review")`, and returns the input physical id only when it is one of those computed values.'
Current implementation takes no repo_root and returns None for any id not in the hardcoded {code-review-base, code-review-verify} ∪ {code-review-<overlay>} set.

The plan does not say what happens when repo_root=None: does the function fall back to the old closed-set validation, return None, or raise? The existing 11 overlay ids must still resolve without a repo.
findings.finding: The plan's prescribed `_code_review_prompt_id(prompt_id, repo_root)` extension does not specify the fallback behavior when `repo_root=None` — whether it falls back to the current closed-set check (base/verify/overlays) or fails entirely — leaving an unspecified sub-step fallback for a caller without a repo root.
scenarios: A test or CI caller invokes `run_case('code-review-security', case, runner=r, repo_root=None)` after the change — without a fallback path it would fail to recognize the built-in overlay id.
The eval regression suite (test_code_review_evals.py) likely passes repo_root=None for built-in overlays; if the fallback is wrong, the suite breaks.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a decision rule co-located with the runner's provenance-tagging logic specifying: for project-prompt findings, `criteria` is unconditionally overwritten with `[logical_id]` and `reviewer_id` is unconditionally set (not setdefault) to the physical prompt id; for built-in findings, both fields are unchanged (setdefault behavior preserved).
evidence: Plan: 'On each emitted project finding, overwrite `criteria` with `[logical_id]` and set `reviewer_id` to the physical prompt id. Pass 3 therefore consumes only logical routing keys regardless of model output; built-in finding attribution remains unchanged.'
batch_runner.py:79: `f.setdefault('reviewer_id', prompt_id)` — this is setdefault (no overwrite), and there is no `criteria` overwrite at all.

The plan says built-in attribution 'remains unchanged' — this is a correctness constraint, but the implementation protocol (conditional on project vs. built-in) is only stated in prose and not as a decision rule co-located with the schema it modifies.
findings.finding: The plan mandates overwriting each project finding's `criteria` with `[logical_id]` and `reviewer_id` with the physical prompt id, but the existing `CodeReviewBatchRunner` uses `f.setdefault('reviewer_id', prompt_id)` (never overwrites). The plan does not co-locate the overwrite-vs-setdefault decision rule with the runner's provenance-tagging logic, leaving ambiguity about whether built-in findings also get overwritten or only project findings.
findings.location: Scope section, paragraph 7: 'For every project prompt, retain the local physical-to-logical map built during fan-in...' / src/rebar/llm/code_review/batch_runner.py:77-80
scenarios: If the implementer applies the overwrite unconditionally (to all findings including built-ins), built-in criteria fields in findings get replaced, breaking Pass-3 routing for built-in criteria.
If reviewer_id is set via setdefault for project findings (matching the current pattern), a model that sets reviewer_id itself would prevent the physical prompt id from being recorded.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that the dual-gate CLI preflight extension preserves existing plan-review-only criterion id acceptance (e.g. `F1`) and add an explicit test case for this in `test_code_review_project_criteria.py` under `-k cli`.
evidence: Current code: `from rebar.llm.plan_review import registry; if args.criterion_id not in registry.by_id(repo_root):` — rejects everything not in plan-review registry
Child scope: 'Compare membership in plan-review and code-review registries: no match is unknown, exactly one selects that gate, and a dual-gate match is an explicit ambiguity error'
Child AC4: tests 'unknown or inactive ids plus a dual-gate ambiguous id are rejected' — covers new paths

Neither child scope nor child ACs explicitly state that the plan-review-only rejection path remains intact after extension (i.e. a built-in plan-review criterion still works via the updated preflight)
container pairing: parent + 8a4b-e8b6-1bf9-48d8
findings.finding: The `criteria eval` CLI preflight in `_llm_eval_commands.py` currently consults only `plan_review.registry.by_id(repo_root)` and will reject any code-review project id (e.g. `project.review-phase-boundaries`) as 'unknown', producing the wrong error path. Both parent and child specify adding a dual-gate membership check, but the child's AC4 verify command (`-k 'eval or cli'`) must cover rejection of a code-review id through the *old* plan-review-only path as a regression, yet neither child AC nor the child scope explicitly calls out backward-compatibility of the plan-review-only rejection path after the dual-gate extension.
scenarios: After the dual-gate extension, a caller passes a built-in plan-review id (e.g. 'F1') through `rebar criteria eval`. If the extended preflight iterates both registries and the code-review registry also happens to contain 'F1' as a coincidental overlay name, it could misfire the dual-gate ambiguity error.

If the test suite only exercises the new code-review paths and does not regression-test plan-review-only ids through the extended preflight, the child AC5 regression suite is the only backstop — which is correct but not explicitly named.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a child AC or a note in the implementation scope stating: 'the dual-gate preflight preserves plan-review-only id acceptance; a test case `test_cli_plan_review_id_still_accepted` (tagged `cli`) confirms `F1` resolves through the plan-review arm without ambiguity error.'

findings.finding: AC #6's documentation requirement ('docs/llm-framework.md documents the completed code-review project-criterion runtime') has no automated verifier — the supplied command (`pytest ... && make lint`) checks code correctness and style but does not confirm that the documentation was actually updated or covers the required topics.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the AC #6 verify command: `&& grep -q 'project-criterion runtime' docs/llm-framework.md && grep -q 'configuration failure' docs/llm-framework.md` so the check fails if the required content is absent.

findings.finding: The plan says '`calibrate_criterion` receives the resolved gate key and calls the same mapper' but the current `calibrate_criterion` signature in `eval.py` has no `gate_key` parameter and always calls `criterion_prompt_id(criterion_id)` (plan-review prefix). The plan does not specify the new function signature, the call site in `_criteria_eval`, or how the gate key flows from the CLI preflight's gate-selection logic through to `calibrate_criterion`.
findings.location: Scope section: 'calibrate_criterion receives the resolved gate key and calls the same mapper'; src/rebar/llm/evals/eval.py calibrate_criterion lines 384-489; src/rebar/_cli/_llm_eval_commands.py _criteria_eval lines 124-183
findings.suggested_fix: Add `gate_key: str = 'plan_review'` to `calibrate_criterion` and update the prompt_id derivation to `criterion_prompt_id(criterion_id, gate_key=gate_key)`. Update `_criteria_eval` to pass the resolved gate key.

findings.finding: The plan says a project-criterion finding 'retains its logical criterion id', and Pass-3 `threshold_for` looks up the `criteria` list field of each finding against the routing map (whose keys are LOGICAL ids like `project.review-phase-boundaries`). However, neither the plan nor the existing code specifies any protocol for ensuring that a project-criterion's findings carry the LOGICAL id in their `criteria` field. The `CodeReviewBatchRunner` only sets `reviewer_id` to the PHYSICAL prompt id; the `criteria` field is what the LLM emits. If the LLM emits the physical prompt id (or anything other than the logical id) in `criteria`, `threshold_for` will find no routing entry and silently fall back to the 0.95 default — ignoring the configured block_threshold and blocking_enabled values.
findings.location: Scope section: 'retains its logical criterion id'; AC line 2: 'retains its logical criterion id'; src/rebar/llm/code_review/batch_runner.py lines 77-79; src/rebar/llm/code_review/registry.py threshold_for lines 309-332; src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/decide.py pass3_over_findings lines 421-422

findings.finding: Parent AC2 requires that a synthetic project criterion 'retains its logical criterion id' in findings — a distinct observable outcome — but child AC1 only asserts that the project prompt 'runs once across same-runner round_a and round_b requests'; neither child AC1 nor any other child AC explicitly tests or asserts logical-id retention on emitted findings. This is a partial-without-remainder gap.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an assertion to `test_code_review_project_criteria.py::test_fan_in` (or a new `test_fan_in_logical_id` case) that inspects the criterion_id field of returned findings and confirms it matches the logical `project.<name>` form, plus a check that the resolved prompt id matches the expected sanitized physical form.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the exact closure shape for binding `effective_routing` into the `threshold_for` callback, e.g. `lambda criteria: registry.threshold_for(criteria, routing)` or `functools.partial(registry.threshold_for, routing_map=routing)`, so the kernel's single-argument call site is satisfied.
evidence: Plan: 'Bind the kernel's threshold callback to `registry.threshold_for(criterion, routing)`—which returns both `block_threshold` and `blocking_enabled`'
workflow_ops.py:419: `threshold_for=registry.threshold_for` — currently passed as a bare function reference.
registry.py:309-332: `threshold_for(criteria, routing_map=None)` takes `criteria` as first arg and `routing_map` as optional second.
review_kernel/decide.py:422: `block_threshold, blocking_enabled = threshold_for(f.get('criteria', []))` — the kernel calls threshold_for with ONE argument (criteria only).

To thread `routing` into the callback, the implementer must write `lambda criteria: registry.threshold_for(criteria, routing)` or equivalent — the plan's phrase 'bind... to `registry.threshold_for(criterion, routing)`' reads as a direct call, not a closure.
findings.finding: The plan says to bind `code_review_decide`'s `threshold_for` callback to `registry.threshold_for(criterion, routing)` using the computed `effective_routing`, but the kernel expects a `ThresholdResolver` callable `(criteria: Sequence[str]) -> (float, bool)` — the plan does not specify the closure/currying shape needed to thread `routing` into the callback without changing the kernel's call site.
findings.location: Scope section, paragraph 9: 'In `code_review_decide`, compute `routing = registry.effective_routing(ctx.repo_root)` once. Bind the kernel's threshold callback to `registry.threshold_for(criterion, routing)`' / src/rebar/llm/code_review/workflow_ops.py:416-421
scenarios: An implementer reads 'bind the callback to `registry.threshold_for(criterion, routing)`' and passes `registry.threshold_for(criteria, routing)` (the result of calling it) rather than a lambda, which would pass a tuple where a callable is expected, causing a runtime TypeError.
Alternatively the implementer uses `functools.partial` but curries the wrong argument position.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the error class for the ambiguous-gate case (e.g. `EvalError` or a new `AmbiguousGateError(LLMError)`) so the CLI's existing `except LLMError` handler catches it and prints a clean message.
evidence: Plan: 'two matches fail with an explicit ambiguous-gate error rather than silently choosing a prompt'
The CLI's existing `_criteria_eval` catches `LLMError` and prints it; a `CriteriaError` (subclass of `Exception`, not `LLMError`) would fall through unhandled.
eval.py:50: `class EvalError(LLMError)` — the eval path uses EvalError.
The plan does not specify which error class is raised for the ambiguous-gate case, so an implementer cannot know whether to catch it in the existing `except LLMError` block or add a new handler.
This is an unspecified fallback protocol for a named sub-step (dual-gate detection) that must surface cleanly to the user.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new 'explicit ambiguous-gate error' for a criterion id active in both plan-review and code-review gates, but does not name the error class to raise — it is unclear whether this is a `CriteriaError`, `LLMError`, `EvalError`, or a plain `ValueError` surfaced via the CLI's stderr path.
findings.location: Scope section, paragraph 5: 'two matches fail with an explicit ambiguous-gate error rather than silently choosing a prompt' / src/rebar/_cli/_llm_eval_commands.py:124-155
scenarios: If the ambiguous-gate error is raised as `CriteriaError` (not a subclass of LLMError), the CLI's `except LLMError` handler does not catch it, causing an unhandled exception and a Python traceback instead of a clean error message.
If it is raised as a plain `ValueError`, the same problem occurs.
findings.suggested_fix: Raise `EvalError(f'criterion {cid!r} is active in both plan_review and code_review gates; pass --gate to disambiguate')` so the existing `except LLMError` handler in `_criteria_eval` catches it.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
